courses.title: Thermodynamics II — Cycles & Utilities
courses.summary: The plant utilities course wearing a thermodynamics badge: steam cycles, gas turbines, refrigeration, compressed air, and psychrometrics — sized, audited, and read from the utility deck of a real factory.
taught_from: Çengel-tradition cycle analysis via MIT OCW 2.005/2.006 materials

lessons.scope: The ideal Rankine cycle on T–s coordinates with each device audited by the steady-flow energy equation. Efficiency levers: pressure, superheat, condenser vacuum. The backbone of every steam utility system.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Trace steam through the four Rankine devices on a T–s diagram.
Why does condenser vacuum matter so much to cycle efficiency?

lessons.t: Real steam cycles: reheat, regeneration, losses
lessons.scope: Reheat and feedwater heating and why plants pay for them. Component irreversibilities located on the h–s diagram. Heat rate as the operator's efficiency language.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: What does reheat buy besides efficiency?
Why does feedwater heating raise cycle efficiency at all?

lessons.scope: Boilers, headers, PRVs, steam traps, and condensate return as one thermodynamic system. Steam quality, flash steam, and trap failure modes costed with the SFEE. The utilities lesson KDD-scale food plants and EQUATE-scale process plants both live by.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: A trap fails open on a 10 bar header: name the two distinct loss mechanisms it creates.
Why is hot condensate worth far more returned than its water value alone?
Explain flash steam: why does condensate partially vaporize crossing into low pressure?

lessons.scope: Brayton cycle with real component efficiencies. Pressure ratio trade-offs; intercooling and regeneration in outline. The prime mover behind Gulf power and large compression trains.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Why does gas-turbine efficiency climb with pressure ratio — and what stops it?
What fraction-scale of turbine work does the compressor consume, and why does it matter?

lessons.scope: The refrigeration cycle on P–h coordinates; COP computed from enthalpies. Superheat and subcooling as health indicators. Chillers and cold stores — refrigeration as a food plant's beating heart.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Read a refrigeration cycle on P–h: where are the four processes?
Why do superheat and subcooling numbers diagnose charge and health?

lessons.scope: Moist-air properties and the psychrometric chart worked fluently. Cooling-and-dehumidification processes traced. Gulf-summer design conditions and what they do to equipment ratings.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: What two properties fix a moist-air state on the psychrometric chart?
Why does Gulf summer humidity punish cooling coils twice?

lessons.t: Cooling towers and cooling water systems
lessons.scope: Evaporative cooling read straight off the psychrometric chart: range, approach, and why wet-bulb temperature sets the floor. Cycles of concentration, makeup, and blowdown arithmetic. The condenser cooling loop as the plant's other utility — sized, fouled, and chemically managed.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Why can a cooling tower cool water below the ambient dry-bulb temperature?
What does raising cycles of concentration save, and what does it risk?

lessons.scope: Compression work, multistaging, and intercooling derived and priced. Receivers, dryers, and dew point. Leak economics — why compressed air is the plant's most expensive utility per joule.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Why does multistaging with intercooling approach isothermal compression?
Price a 1 mm leak conceptually: what makes compressed air so expensive?

lessons.scope: Stoichiometry, excess air, and flue-gas analysis. Heating values and combustion efficiency from stack measurements. Boiler tuning as applied chemistry.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: What does excess air do for combustion and against efficiency?
Which flue-gas measurements reveal a burner running rich?

lessons.scope: Second-law accounting applied to boilers, heat exchangers, and throttles. Exergy destruction rankings as an audit's priority list. Where the utility bill actually leaks.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Why is a throttle an exergy bonfire while being energy-neutral?
Rank boiler, exchanger, throttle by typical exergy destruction.

lessons.scope: A guided paper audit of a representative factory utility deck: steam, air, chilled water. Balances closed, losses ranked, fixes proposed with payback estimates. The Tier-2 utilities lesson practiced end to end.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Applied Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — applied thermo
preview: Which three utility systems does a factory audit balance first, and why?
What makes a payback claim from a utility audit credible?

courses.career:
Thermodynamics II is the plant-utilities course wearing a cycles textbook's clothes — the sector picks which utility dominates:

  • Power generation and steam turbines — utilities and performance engineers at turbine manufacturers such as Mitsubishi Power size and tune exactly the Rankine cycle, reheat, and isentropic-efficiency accounting of Lessons 1-2 — heat rate, not raw efficiency, is the number they report daily, exactly as Lesson 2 teaches.
  • Industrial refrigeration and HVAC — applications engineers at manufacturers such as Carrier build the chillers and cooling-coil equipment behind Lesson 5's COP and Lesson 6's sensible/latent split — a Gulf-climate cooling load genuinely running close to half latent is exactly the design condition their equipment is rated against.
  • Compressed air systems — applications engineers at manufacturers such as Atlas Copco build the multistage, intercooled compressors of Lesson 8 — the isothermal-vs-adiabatic work trade-off and leak-cost economics this course derives are the same numbers their own sizing and audit tools compute.

Interview angle: the classic probe is an audit scenario — "here is a utility deck with N loss sources, rank them and propose a fix with payback" — expecting the same close-the-balance, rank-by-common-basis, cost-with-payback method this course's own capstone project practices, not a single memorized efficiency number.

courses.title: Heat Transfer
courses.summary: Conduction, convection, radiation, and the exchangers that trade them — with the transient conduction that casting already used, and the fouling economics every process plant argues about.
taught_from: Çengel & Ghajar, Heat and Mass Transfer: Fundamentals and Applications (McGraw-Hill)

lessons.scope: Conduction, convection, and radiation rate equations side by side. Thermal resistance networks for walls and cylinders. Insulation economics and the critical-radius surprise.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 1–3; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Series thermal resistances: which layer dominates and why?
What does the critical insulation radius warn about thin pipes?

lessons.scope: The fin equation derived and solved; efficiency and effectiveness. Finned surfaces as area multipliers on air-cooled equipment. When fins stop paying.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 3; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Why do fins have diminishing returns with length?
Fin efficiency versus effectiveness: what does each measure?

lessons.scope: Lumped capacitance with its Biot-number entry ticket, then the semi-infinite solid and one-term chart solutions. Heating and quench times estimated honestly. The physics already met in Chvorinov's rule, now in its own right.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 4; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Biot number 0.05 for a quenched pin: is lumped capacitance legitimate, and what does τ = ρVc/hA mean physically?
Why does the surface heat flux into a semi-infinite solid fall as 1/√t?
Roughly how does doubling a plate's thickness change its one-term-model cooling time?

lessons.scope: Boundary layers and the Nusselt–Reynolds–Prandtl grammar. Flat plates, cylinders, and tube banks by correlation. Using correlations without believing them past their range.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 7; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: What do Nusselt, Reynolds, and Prandtl each encode?
Why does a correlation's validity range matter?

lessons.scope: Developing versus developed flow; constant-flux and constant-temperature results. The Dittus–Boelter workhorse with its fine print. Heat pickup in real piping runs.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 8; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Constant flux versus constant wall temperature: which internal-flow result applies to a steam-jacketed pipe?
What does 'fully developed' mean thermally?

lessons.scope: Grashof and Rayleigh numbers; vertical plates and enclosures. Hot cabinets and un-fanned motors in Gulf ambient. Why still summer air is an equipment rating problem.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 9; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Why does natural convection depend on orientation?
What does Rayleigh number decide?

lessons.scope: The boiling curve and critical heat flux respected properly. Film condensation on tubes. The phase-change coefficients that make steam equipment compact.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 10; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Why is critical heat flux a safety limit, not a performance target?
Film versus dropwise condensation: which do real plants get?

lessons.scope: Shell-and-tube and plate architectures; overall coefficient built from resistances, fouling included. LMTD design and effectiveness-NTU rating methods, each derived, each worked once. Fouling monitoring as the maintenance engineer's stake in this lesson.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 11; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Counterflow exchanger: hot stream 150→90 °C, cold 30→70 °C. Compute the LMTD.
Fouling resistance doubles: what happens to U and to the outlet temperatures, qualitatively?
When is effectiveness-NTU the only honest method, and why does LMTD fail there?

lessons.scope: Blackbody laws, emissivity, and view factors in simple geometries; two-surface enclosures and network-method basics. Radiation shields, and combined convection–radiation surface losses. Estimating bare-pipe losses the way an energy audit does — the physics the furnace and the thermographer's camera share.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 12–13; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: Why does emissivity dominate radiation exchange for gray surfaces?
Estimate bare-pipe loss: which two modes add, and when does radiation win?

lessons.scope: Diffusion and convective mass transfer via the heat-transfer analogy. Evaporation and drying estimates. The shared mathematics made explicit.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 14; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: State the heat–mass transfer analogy in one sentence.
Why does evaporation cool the liquid left behind?

lessons.scope: Rate or size a small exchanger against a duty with fouling margins, then critique the design's failure modes. Calculations packaged for review. The heat-transfer counterpart of the shaft project.
lessons.src: Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 11; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — heat transfer
preview: In the exchanger mini-project, which resistance dominated, and what would you attack first?
What fouling margin did you carry, and how does it show on the datasheet?

courses.career:
Heat Transfer is the course underneath almost every piece of thermal equipment a mechanical engineer will ever spec, inspect, or troubleshoot — the sector picks which side of the transfer dominates:

  • Process heat exchangers — thermal design and rating engineers at exchanger manufacturers such as Alfa Laval live in Lesson 8's LMTD and effectiveness–NTU methods daily, and Lesson 11's fouling-margin arithmetic is literally how a clean-service duty becomes an installed, warranted design.
  • Industrial refrigeration and HVAC controls — applications engineers at manufacturers such as Danfoss size the evaporators, condensers, and expansion devices Lesson 7's boiling curve and film-condensation constants describe, and Lesson 6's natural-convection derating shapes how their equipment is rated for a Gulf-summer ambient.
  • Electronics and data-center thermal management — a fast-growing mechanical-engineering specialty at companies such as Vertiv, where Lesson 2's fin effectiveness and Lesson 4's forced-air correlations are the daily arithmetic behind every heat sink and cold-aisle airflow design.

Interview angle: the classic probe is a mechanism-identification question — "here is a hot surface, or a failing cooler: name every heat-transfer mode in play and rank them" — expecting the same resistance-network, order-of-magnitude thinking this course built from Lesson 1 onward, not a single memorized formula.

courses.title: Machine Design I
courses.summary: From stress analysis to sized hardware: static and fatigue design methodology, shafts, keys, fasteners, and welded joints — the course where strength of materials starts buying parts.
taught_from: MIT OCW 2.72 Elements of Mechanical Design (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.t: Design methodology and factors of safety
lessons.scope: Load cases, failure modes, and design margins chosen with reasons attached. Codes and standards as encoded experience. The difference between analysis and design stated plainly.
preview: Why is a factor of safety a statement about ignorance, not strength?
Analysis versus design: what reverses between them?

lessons.scope: Von Mises and Tresca applied to component sizing. Stress concentrations included honestly. A bracket designed from load to drawing callout.
preview: Apply von Mises to a bracket's worst point: what inputs do you need?
Why include Kt in static design of brittle parts but often not ductile ones?

lessons.t: Fatigue design I: the S–N method
lessons.scope: Endurance limits and the modification factors — surface, size, load, temperature, reliability. Where the knockdowns come from and why they stack. Infinite-life design for steel done properly.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 8
preview: Name three endurance-limit knockdown factors and what each punishes.
Why does a ground surface out-live a machined one in fatigue?

lessons.t: Fatigue design II: mean stress and cumulative damage
lessons.scope: Goodman and Gerber treatments of mean stress. Miner's rule with its known dishonesty acknowledged. Fluctuating-load components assessed end to end.
preview: What does the Goodman line trade between mean and alternating stress?
State Miner's rule and its known dishonesty.

lessons.scope: Combined bending and torsion under fatigue; deflection and critical-speed checks. Shoulders, keyways, and their concentration costs. A pump shaft sized and detailed — the pathway's signature component.
preview: Which loads combine on a pump shaft, and which failure mode usually governs?
Why do shoulders and keyways decide a shaft's fatigue life?

lessons.scope: Key sizing by shear and bearing. Splines and interference fits as torque paths. Retaining rings and their groove-stress fine print.
preview: Size a key: which two failure checks apply?
When does an interference fit beat a key for torque?

lessons.scope: Bolt mechanics: preload, joint stiffness, and the load-sharing triangle. Torque–preload relations and their scatter. Gasketed-joint implications for every flange a plant owns.
preview: Why does preload protect a bolt from fatigue?
What fraction of external load does a stiff joint's bolt actually see?

lessons.scope: Fillet weld sizing under direct and torsional shear. Weld groups treated like bolt groups. Fatigue of welds — why the toe is where cracks start.
preview: Why do fillet welds fail at the throat, and what area do you size?
Why do weld toes start fatigue cracks?

lessons.scope: Helical spring stress and rate derived; solid height and buckling checks. Fatigue of springs under fluctuating load. Valve and relief springs as working examples.
preview: What stress does a helical spring's wire actually carry?
What does solid height check against?

lessons.scope: Torque to raise and lower; efficiency and self-locking conditions derived from the friction analysis. Acme threads and collar friction. Jacks, actuators, and valve stems quantified.
preview: Derive when a power screw self-locks.
Why is a screw jack's efficiency so poor — and why is that useful?

lessons.t: Design project: a driven shaft assembly
lessons.scope: Integrative project: size the shaft, keys, bolts, and welds of a belt-driven assembly against static and fatigue criteria. Calculations documented for review. The pattern of defensible design work.
preview: In the shaft-assembly project, which element failed first on paper, and what did you change?
What must design documentation let a reviewer do?

courses.title: Kinematics & Dynamics of Machinery
courses.summary: Mechanisms in motion: linkages, cams, gear geometry and trains, flywheels, and machine balancing — how rotation becomes useful motion, and where the shaking forces that vibration analysts chase are born.

lessons.scope: Links, joints, and kinematic chains; Grübler's mobility count. Four-bar and slider-crank as the industrial workhorses. Inversions and what each one becomes.
preview: Count mobility for a four-bar: what does DOF = 1 buy?
Name two inversions of the slider-crank and their machines.

lessons.scope: Graphical and analytical position solutions. Velocity by instant centers and by vector loops. Reading mechanism speed ratios without simulation.
preview: Locate instant centers of a four-bar: what do they give you instantly?
Why does a mechanism's speed ratio vary through its cycle?

lessons.scope: Acceleration polygons and the Coriolis component in sliding contacts. Slider-crank acceleration solved completely. The source terms for inertia forces.
preview: Where does Coriolis acceleration appear in a quick-return mechanism?
Why does acceleration, not velocity, size inertia forces?

lessons.scope: Follower motion laws — uniform, harmonic, cycloidal — and their acceleration signatures. Profile synthesis and pressure angle. Why cam wear patterns encode their motion law. Filling-machine cams as the local example.
preview: Why does cycloidal motion beat harmonic for high-speed cams?
What does pressure angle limit in a cam-follower?

lessons.scope: The involute and why conjugate action matters. Module, pressure angle, and interference. Backlash as both necessity and defect.
preview: Why does the involute tolerate center-distance error?
What does interference between teeth physically destroy?

lessons.scope: Simple, compound, and planetary trains; ratio arithmetic including the tabular planetary method. Gearbox ratios decomposed. The gear-mesh frequencies that will reappear in vibration spectra.
preview: Compute a compound train's ratio: what multiplies?
Why do planetary trains need the tabular method?

lessons.scope: Force transmission through mechanisms; free-body chains under motion. Equivalent offset inertia forces. Bearing loads born from acceleration, not just weight.
preview: Why do accelerating links load bearings beyond gravity?
What is an equivalent offset inertia force?

lessons.scope: Friction torque of plate and cone clutches under uniform-pressure and uniform-wear assumptions — and which one to trust in service. Band and shoe brakes with self-energizing behavior located. The heat released in every engagement, and where it goes.
preview: Uniform pressure versus uniform wear: which predicts less torque, and why is it the honest one for a worn clutch?
What makes a brake shoe self-energizing — and when does that become self-locking?

lessons.scope: Turning-moment diagrams and coefficient of fluctuation. Flywheel sizing from energy variation. Presses and compressors as the plants' flywheel machines.
preview: Read a turning-moment diagram: what does the shaded area mean?
What does the coefficient of fluctuation specify?

lessons.t: Balancing of rotating and reciprocating masses
lessons.scope: Single- and multi-plane rotating balance done with vectors. Reciprocating unbalance and why it can't be fully balanced. The kinematic truth behind field balancing jobs.
preview: Why can rotating unbalance be fully balanced but reciprocating not?
Two-plane balancing: why two?

lessons.scope: Centrifugal governors as proportional controllers made of iron. Gyroscopic couples on turbine and vehicle bearings. Legacy topics that still explain modern behavior.
preview: What does a governor control that a flywheel cannot?
Where do gyroscopic couples load a turbine's bearings?

courses.title: Metrology & Quality Control
courses.summary: Measurement you can defend: instruments, uncertainty, calibration, GD&T verification, and the SPC toolkit — the quality half of a production engineer's day job.
taught_from: NPTEL: Metrology and Quality Control-style courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Accuracy, precision, resolution, and bias untangled for good. Traceability chains to national standards. The vocabulary that makes calibration certificates readable.
preview: Accuracy versus precision: sketch the four target patterns.
What does traceability connect your caliper to?

lessons.scope: Calipers, micrometers, dial indicators, gauge blocks, and comparators — use, care, and abuse. Abbe error and why instrument geometry matters. Temperature effects at the micron level.
preview: Why does Abbe error make a caliper worse than a micrometer?
What does 20 °C have to do with a certified gauge block?

lessons.scope: Uncertainty budgets built from type A and B components. Calibration intervals and out-of-tolerance investigations. A micrometer calibration worked end to end.
preview: Type A versus Type B uncertainty: what distinguishes them?
Out-of-tolerance found at calibration: what must the investigation cover?

lessons.scope: Measuring flatness, roundness, and runout — the rotating-equipment repair set. Verifying position with datums. CMM measurement in outline.
preview: Measure runout on a shaft: setup, instrument, and what the number means.
What does a datum system add to a position check?

lessons.scope: Limits and fits recalled from the drawing course, then verified the shop-floor way: GO/NO-GO limit gauges designed with gauge-maker's tolerance. Taylor's principle — why the GO gauge must check the whole feature while the NO-GO checks point by point. Where attribute gauging beats measurement, and where it hides trouble.
preview: Why must a GO gauge check the whole feature while the NO-GO checks point by point?
Gauge-maker's tolerance sits inside the work tolerance: who does that protect?

lessons.scope: Ra, Rz, and the stylus trace read properly. Cutoffs and filtering honestly explained. Finish specs matched to gaskets, seals, and bearing seats.
preview: Why does cutoff length change a measured Ra?
Match a finish spec to a mechanical-seal face versus a painted guard.

lessons.scope: Variation: common versus special cause — the distinction all of SPC rests on. X̄–R charts built and read. Control limits versus specification limits, never confused again.
preview: Common versus special cause: why does the response differ?
Why are control limits never the same thing as spec limits?

lessons.t: Statistical process control II and capability
lessons.scope: Attribute charts in brief; capability indices Cp and Cpk computed and abused-proofed. What Cpk = 1.33 actually promises. Capability studies as pre-production gatekeeping.
preview: Cp = 1.8 but Cpk = 0.7: what is the process doing?
What does Cpk = 1.33 promise in defect terms, roughly?

lessons.scope: Sampling plans, OC curves, and the risks each party carries. AQL semantics without the folklore. Where sampling makes sense and where 100% inspection is owed.
preview: What do producer's and consumer's risk mean on an OC curve?
When is 100% inspection actually owed?

lessons.scope: Repeatability and reproducibility studies: can the gauge see the tolerance? %GRR computed and judged. The study that should precede every SPC deployment and rarely does.
preview: %GRR at 35%: what does that verdict mean for your SPC plans?
Repeatability versus reproducibility: whose variation is each?

lessons.scope: ISO 9001 logic: say it, do it, prove it. Nonconformance, CAPA, and audit trails. Where quality systems touch maintenance records — more often than either side admits.
preview: Say-do-prove: map ISO 9001's logic onto a calibration program.
Where do maintenance records enter a quality audit?

courses.title: Manufacturing Processes III — Sheet, Plastics & Additive
courses.summary: The discrete-products half of manufacturing: sheet-metal working with springback treated properly, plastics processing, packaging operations, and additive — Kirby's panel lines and KDD's filling halls as the anchors.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing (Wiley) — sheet metal & plastics chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology (Pearson)

lessons.scope: Shearing, blanking, and punching; clearance and its burr signature. Cutting force estimates. Nesting and material yield as quiet profit levers.
preview: Why does punch-die clearance set burr height?
What does nesting efficiency do to material cost?

lessons.scope: Bend allowance from neutral-axis geometry; bending force; and springback — why it happens, how much to expect, and the compensation strategies press brakes actually use. Kirby's roll-formed and brake-formed building panels as the standing example.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 20; Kalpakjian & Schmid, sheet-forming chapter
preview: Compute bend allowance for a 90° bend, R = 6 mm, t = 3 mm.
Springback scales with which material ratio — and why does high-strength steel spring back more than mild steel?
V-die bending force: what happens to required tonnage when sheet thickness doubles?

lessons.scope: Drawability and the limiting draw ratio; blankholder force and defect map — wrinkling versus tearing. Redraws and ironing. Cans and cookware as engineered products.
preview: What limits the drawing ratio in one hit?
Wrinkling versus tearing: which control fixes each?

lessons.t: Roll forming and high-volume sheet lines
lessons.scope: Progressive roll forming of profiles; line balancing and tooling. Coil handling, leveling, and slitting upstream. The continuous logic of Kirby-style panel production.
preview: Why does roll forming beat brake bending at volume?
What does the flower pattern encode?

lessons.scope: Mechanical and hydraulic presses; die sets and progressive dies. Tonnage curves and where presses are overloaded. Die maintenance as a production-uptime discipline.
preview: Mechanical versus hydraulic press: where does each win?
Why is die maintenance a production KPI?

lessons.scope: Screw extruder anatomy and melt behavior. Die swell and downstream sizing. Cable insulation extrusion — Gulf Cable's polymer line — as the worked context.
preview: What does die swell reveal about polymer melts?
Why is cable insulation extruded over a moving conductor rather than moulded?

lessons.scope: The molding cycle: fill, pack, cool, eject. Gate and runner logic; shrinkage and warpage. Defects — shorts, flash, sinks — mapped to their process causes.
preview: Map short shot, flash, and sink marks to their process causes.
Why does gate location decide warpage?

lessons.scope: Bottles, trays, and films — the packaging trio. Parison control and wall-thickness distribution. Food-grade material and hygiene constraints introduced.
preview: Why does parison control set bottle wall thickness?
Which food-contact constraints shape material choice?

lessons.scope: Filling, capping, labeling, date-coding, and case packing as a machine system; line speed, accumulation, and the V-curve logic that protects the filler. Hygienic design and changeover discipline. The KDD-style dairy line read as one machine.
preview: Why is the filler the protected center of a packaging line, and what does the V-curve of speeds around it do?
Name two hygienic-design features that separate food-line equipment from generic machinery.
Why does changeover reduction (SMED) buy more capacity on packaging lines than almost anywhere else?

lessons.scope: PBF, DED, material extrusion, and binder jetting with their materials and defect signatures. Post-processing realities. Where AM fits a maintenance organization: fixtures and unobtainable spares.
preview: Rank PBF, FDM, binder jetting by surface finish and by strength honesty.
What post-processing does each AM family assume?

lessons.scope: DFM/DFA rules that move cost: part-count reduction, self-locating features, tolerance realism. Redesign exercises on real hardware. The conversation between design and the floor formalized.
preview: Give two DFM rules that remove cost without removing function.
Why does part-count reduction beat per-part cheapening?

videos.title: SME — forming, plastics, and additive process videos
videos.note: Sheet forming, molding, and additive explainers from the manufacturing professional body.

videos.title: Manufacturing lectures by Eng. Mahmoud Marzouk
videos.note: Arabic-language manufacturing-processes lectures (casting, forming, sheet metal). English sources above remain the primary path.
